results.question: What is management's volume and margin guidance for the core business divisions?
results.expected_answer: Management guided FY26 consolidated revenue of Rs.7,000 crores, EBITDA of Rs.1,100 crores, and EBIT in the range of Rs.650-700 crores (depreciation ~Rs.450 crores). Craftsman's own conventional business capacity utilization was 'slightly below 70%' with upper limit of 85-90%; aluminium business (Sunbeam/DR Axion) operating at ~70-75%, expected to touch 80-85% by Q2 FY26. Sunbeam standalone: blended EBITDA guided at 8-10% for FY26 (Q1 weak, Q4 strongest), with revenue guided flat at ~Rs.1,200 crores. Craftsman (standalone) organic growth guided at >20% CAGR for FY26-FY27; DR Axion growth guided at 8-10% for FY26 (slightly higher than 10% in FY27).
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- Management confirmed the overall revenue and margin guidance for the company as:
  - Revenue: Around Rs. 7,000 crores
  - EBITDA: Around Rs. 1,100 crores
  - EBIT: Around Rs. 700 crores  
  (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 7 · FY25)

- On volume growth and segment-wise confidence (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 10 · FY25):
  - Management expects 20%+ organic growth driven by diversification of customer base and product mix.
  - Segment revenue mix is approximately:
    - Commercial Vehicle (CV): 17%
    - Two Wheeler: 18%
    - Passenger Vehicle (PV): 32%
    - Storage: 10%
    - Off-Highways: 5%
    - Tractor: 4%
  - Growth confidence is supported by export-oriented customers and new markets like Africa.

- On margins in the Powertrain segment (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 4 · FY25):
  - Current powertrain business margins are strong due to machining value-addition.
  - New powertrain business (larger engines) will initially have castings sourced from Europe/US, which lowers margin on castings but overall blended margins are expected to be in line with current powertrain margins.
  - The margin profile is expected to be stable or improve with ramp-up of foundry capacity in India.

- On recent margin improvement in Powertrain (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25):
  - Margin improvement driven by better operating leverage, higher capacity utilization, and cost optimization.
  - Revival in farm and commercial vehicle sectors is supporting volume and margin stability.
  - Margins are expected to sustain or improve in FY26 compared to Q4FY25.

- For Aluminium and Storage Solutions businesses:
  - Management expressed confidence in improving profitability (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 18 · FY25).
  - Storage solutions business is growing rapidly with broadening sector exposure (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 20 · FY25).

## Summary

Management maintains guidance of approximately Rs. 7,000 crores revenue, Rs. 1,100 crores EBITDA, and Rs. 700 crores EBIT for the core business. They expect 20%+ organic volume growth driven by diversified customer segments across commercial vehicles, passenger vehicles, two-wheelers, storage, off-highways, and tractors. Powertrain margins are expected to sustain or improve due to better operating leverage and cost optimization, with new larger engine business margins blending in line with current levels. Aluminium and Storage Solutions businesses are targeted for improved profitability.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 3,4,7,10 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 18,20 · FY25

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. And then secondly on the guidance, right, we have a very strong guidance on
powertrain, aluminum and even storage business. So, if I tie it up to the underlying be
it CV or PV industry, we may not see such high-volume growth. So, where is this
confidence coming for us to deliver 20%-plus kind of growth in an organic manner?

Management answered:
We have been diligently trying to diversify our customer portfolio. Earlier, we had six
customers giving 50% of our revenue and balance 50% of customers coming from equal
100 customers I would say. Now, the top 60% revenue is coming from 12 customers
and in diversified end customer segments. So, our product mix is like this and the
company side revenue, I mean consolidated if we look at it, the commercial vehicle
portion is coming to 17%, two wheeler is coming to 18%, passenger vehicle is 32%,
storage is 10%, off-highways is 5% and tractor is 4%. I think overall we have diversified
quite well and we have also invested for customers who are export-oriented. Many of
the OEMs from the eastern part of the world like Japan and Korea are also setting their
sights on African market for both passenger car, two-wheeler, pickup trucks, tractors
and all the products which are going to go into Africa in the future, which is the last
market to be developed, the products are similar to India, what India wanted in the last
two decades. But India is migrating to slightly higher products now. So, these products
Page 9 of 11

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. You mentioned in past the margin for the segment can be very good for the
heavy engines part. I just want to understand, I mean this will be more-accretive than
the current margins or how do you see the range of the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, the current business, you may be aware that we are not backward integrated, and
we have excellent top notch foundry partners who are leaders in India and also the
OEMs themselves, three of the OEMs in India, major OEM's are running their own
foundries where we are getting the casting from. So, it's a mixture of job work and also
we buy the castings from our foundry partners. So, it is an assorted margin what you're
seeing on the current powertrain business whereas the new powertrain business for the
larger engines, yes, it will start also with the sort of job work activity, but the castings
are not being produced in India, 95% of them, there is only one or two small suppliers
here. So, we are getting the castings from Europe and US to start with for the activity
in 2027, while we ramp up our foundry capacity here with the prove out also, the
castings must be validated. So, when we are supplying this material, it looks like as if
the margins will be lower than the conventional powertrain business, but I can assure
you that the margins are in line with the current powertrain business as in formula wise
how we calculate. When we are buying the castings from elsewhere there cannot be a
margin on the castings, but we make our castings, it will be there, but the margin on the
castings surely it will be slightly lower than the machining because machining is a value
addition, there is no material cost involved. So, it optically look like a higher margin. So,
on the blended average we are on par with whatever we are currently doing.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. Good to see the improvement in the standalone margin. Sir, particularly in
the powertrain segment we have seen improvement this quarter. What led to the
improvement there? And also can you update how the Kothavadi plant order book is
shaping up for the heavy engines and for the FY26 and '27, how do you see the revenue
trends there?

Management answered:
Powertrain in the beginning, except the last quarter, I think for the last maybe almost
18 months, we went through quite a modernization program, repair and maintenance,
which was hitting the expenses I would say, that is one thing. The second thing is the
operating leverage was quite less, capacity utilization was quite less across the old
lines. New lines also were not ramping up. The customers were slow. So, now overall
slight improvement in the operating leverage has happened. That is helping us totally.
There has been from the multinational companies who set up plants in India they have
started to produce with the one- or two-years delay, I would say. And there is some
slight revival in the farm sector and a slight revival in the commercial vehicle sector and
whatever is the downside we had experiencing in the many quarters that has more or
less stabilized now, so that nothing more and whatever we are able to utilize the plants
slightly better and optimize our cost. These margins will be sustained or improved
depending on the quarter of the next financial year, but for the full financial year, we can
expect a better result on the powertrain, i.e., accumulative result even when compared
to the Q4 result. So, the margins will creep up just like the period before we did this
massive correction on the powertrain. So, any questions on the fundamental powertrain
business, then I will come to the new powertrain business.

results.question: What was the impact of raw material price volatility, and what inventory loss/gain was reported?
results.expected_answer: Discussed qualitatively but no specific figure given for the Q4 FY25 quarter itself — management said 'there is always the question of commodity price aluminum. So, that will also change the top line a little' but did not quantify the impact (Srinivasan Ravi, response to Abhishek K Jain). In an earlier quarter (Q2 FY25 call), when directly asked whether aluminium/raw material price changes impacted that quarter's standalone aluminium margin decline (11.8% vs 13.2% prior quarter), management explicitly stated: 'No, I don't think so. I think aluminium-wise, we are more or less same,' attributing the margin decline instead to Bhiwadi start-up expenses, not to raw material price movements.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Raw material price volatility posed a significant risk to the company’s supply chain and production costs, as highlighted in the sustainability and risk disclosures. The company reported inventory losses related to slow-moving inventory provisions amounting to ₹1.88 crore in FY23, down from ₹4.85 crore the previous year, indicating a reduction in inventory write-downs.

## Details  

- **Raw Material Price Volatility Impact:**  
  From Annual-Report-2025.pdf (Page 70, FY25), raw material price volatility is identified as a material risk affecting supply chain stability and production costs. The company emphasizes sustainable sourcing to minimize this risk and reduce reputational damage.

- **Inventory Loss/Gain:**  
  From Annual-Report-2023.pdf (Page 113, FY23), the provision for slow-moving inventory was ₹1.88 crore in the current year, compared to ₹4.85 crore in the previous year. This provision is recognized as an expense and included in the changes in value of inventories of work-in-progress in the Statement of Profit and Loss.

## Sources  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 70 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2023.pdf · Page 113 · FY23

results.question: What are the capital expenditure (capex) plans and guidance for the current and next fiscal year?
results.expected_answer: For FY26, management guided a group-level CAPEX of Rs.750-800 crores. Of this, Rs.550 crores is allocated to standalone Craftsman. For Sunbeam and DR Axion, management said 'we will take it as it comes' (not fixed). An additional Rs.40-50 crores was flagged for repair/maintenance of old equipment at Fronberg (under evaluation). No further break-up by specific project (e.g., Kothavadi/Bhiwadi individually) was given for FY26 in this transcript.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 11 · FY25), management stated:

- The CAPEX guidance for the group for the next financial year is around Rs. 750 crores to Rs. 800 crores in total.
- They also mentioned a potential land sale in Q3 or Q4 of Sunbeam that could add around Rs. 300 crores, which would aid cash generation but is not part of CAPEX.

No explicit figure for the current fiscal year's actual CAPEX was provided in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The capital expenditure (CAPEX) guidance for the next fiscal year is approximately Rs. 750 to Rs. 800 crores for the entire group. There is no explicit CAPEX figure disclosed for the current fiscal year in the retrieved context.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Page/Sheet: 11 · FY25

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Yes. Hi, sir. Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, I have a couple of questions. One is on the
balance sheet working capital. The OCF saw compression due to some working capital
stretch on this consolidated business. So, what is causing that sir for the year?

Management answered:
I didn't understand the question.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity. Sir, my question is on the guidance. In last quarter you have
guided around Rs.7,000 crores of revenue and EBITDA would be around Rs.1,100
crores and EBIT would be around Rs.700 crores. Do you maintain that guidance in this?

Management answered:
Yes.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Okay, sir. And my last question is on the loan borrowing side. Now we have around
Rs.1,900 Crores kind of debt in books. So, what is your deleveraging plan for the next
two to three years?

Management answered:
So, if you look at it, the cash run rate generation will be quite high even in the next
financial year plus aided by possibly the land sale in Q3 or Q4 of Sunbeam, that will
add around Rs.300 crores plus depending on that market value at that particular point
of time. Our CAPEX guidance as a whole for a group will be only around Rs.750 crores
to Rs.800 crores totally in that region.

Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
The operating cash flow for the full year on consolidated basis saw compression and
that is because of our working capital stretch. So, what is causing that operating cash
flow to compress year-on-year?

Management answered:
Because it's a consolidated basis it's Sunbeam, right. It's not an apple-to-apple
comparison because Sunbeam we had it only for six months, that may be the reason
maybe not being able to compare.

results.question: What capacity additions or plant updates did management detail?
results.expected_answer: Specific figures given: Bhiwadi alloy wheel plant guided at 'around Rs.300 crores plus' revenue for FY26, and Hosur alloy wheel plant at 'around Rs.150 crores' for FY26 (reiterated from prior call); by FY27, each of the two plants (Bhiwadi and Hosur) is expected to contribute 'more than Rs.400 crores' on the top line. Sunbeam FY26 revenue guided at 'around 1,200 crores'. DR Axion Q4 FY25 revenue was Rs.376 crores. The new stationary/heavy-engine powertrain business is targeted to reach $100 million (~Rs.800-850 crores) revenue by FY29/2030, with Kothavadi machining-only revenue (ex-industrial engines) of ~Rs.150 crores expected for the full next financial year.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript sources, management provided the following details on capacity additions and plant updates:

- The Kothavadi plant's phase I is ready and became operational in Q1 FY25. This plant is intended for the new powertrain business, specifically for machining large engine blocks. Trials are ongoing, and orders are in place. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25)

- The machine shop for machining large engine blocks is also ready and operational, supporting the new powertrain business. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25)

- Utilization levels for the conventional powertrain business are around 70%, with an upper limit of 85%-90% due to seasonality and market factors. The aluminum segment is operating at around 70%-75% utilization, with plans to increase to 80%-85% by Q2 FY26, which will require CAPEX for new orders already in the pipeline. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- Management mentioned the need for CAPEX to support the strong order book in the aluminum segment, indicating planned capacity expansion to meet demand. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- There is no mention of new capacity additions in the storage business; rather, the focus is on rationalizing product costing and improving margins. The storage business is described as low CAPEX compared to automotive segments. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 9 · FY25)

- No new business is being taken in the short term as the company is in consolidation mode, and new business revenues are not expected in FY25. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 4 · FY25)

## Summary

Management detailed that the Kothavadi plant phase I and the machine shop for large engine blocks are operational as of Q1 FY25, supporting the new powertrain business. The aluminum segment is operating at 70%-75% capacity with plans to increase utilization to 80%-85% by Q2 FY26, necessitating CAPEX for new orders. No significant new capacity additions were mentioned for the storage business, which remains low CAPEX. The company is currently in consolidation mode with no new business revenue expected in FY25.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 3, 4, 6, 9 · FY25

chunks.content:
Analyst (Ajox Frederick) asked:
Okay. So, basically it's incremental customers and the customers also increasing
capacity, so that's helping you. So, that's –

Management answered:
Second point is on alloy wheel and other parts, we are having an import which was
coming from China. Now, it is getting localized within here. It is not a new growth
requirement in India, but imports are getting replaced by Indian suppliers. That's all.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Got it, sir. Sir, this quarter, the storage, I mean the industrial segment margins have
seen a lot of improvement in Q4. Can you explain what led to the improvement there?
And going ahead for the next year how do you see the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, you may be aware of this, but I would like to reiterate one particular point on the
storage business. This is not at all CAPEX-intensive, there is CAPEX, but compared to
the automotive business on both aluminum and powertrain, this is a negligible CAPEX
I would say and working capital also the total capital employed is quite low. So, even
though margins may be low, once we see the traction what we got in Q4, which will
continue into the next financial year, of course, there can be some blips in quarter-to-
quarter but overall we can expect the results for FY26 will be better than the results of
Q4 of FY25. And the reason for that is the automated storage division is getting new
orders because we have made enough inroads into the market, we have enough
projects which have been implemented to showcase to our new incoming customers
and we are getting the right pricing for the product, #1. #2, I think we also have
rationalized the product costing and also on the static racking we are having good
traction there. So, as a blended Company, I think we have matured as a storage solution
Company. This is a very difficult business. Within five years we have come to a level.
This can be really a standalone also possible storage solution business. Not that I'm
implying anything, no, please don't take it that level, but we have grown this business
to be able to stand on its own feet now.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. Good to see the improvement in the standalone margin. Sir, particularly in
the powertrain segment we have seen improvement this quarter. What led to the
improvement there? And also can you update how the Kothavadi plant order book is
shaping up for the heavy engines and for the FY26 and '27, how do you see the revenue
trends there?

Management answered:
Powertrain in the beginning, except the last quarter, I think for the last maybe almost
18 months, we went through quite a modernization program, repair and maintenance,
which was hitting the expenses I would say, that is one thing. The second thing is the
operating leverage was quite less, capacity utilization was quite less across the old
lines. New lines also were not ramping up. The customers were slow. So, now overall
slight improvement in the operating leverage has happened. That is helping us totally.
There has been from the multinational companies who set up plants in India they have
started to produce with the one- or two-years delay, I would say. And there is some
slight revival in the farm sector and a slight revival in the commercial vehicle sector and
whatever is the downside we had experiencing in the many quarters that has more or
less stabilized now, so that nothing more and whatever we are able to utilize the plants
slightly better and optimize our cost. These margins will be sustained or improved
depending on the quarter of the next financial year, but for the full financial year, we can
expect a better result on the powertrain, i.e., accumulative result even when compared
to the Q4 result. So, the margins will creep up just like the period before we did this
massive correction on the powertrain. So, any questions on the fundamental powertrain
business, then I will come to the new powertrain business.

Analyst (Mitul Shah) asked:
Sir, thank you for giving the opportunity, Sir, my first question is if you can help with the
utilization of various business segments currently for Q4?

Management answered:
I think, yes, on the conventional powertrain business, we have touched around 70%-
odd. So, I think the upper limit will be 85% to 90% because of the seasonality as well
as there will be some customers losing market share or they will have some inventory
correction, which will happen. So, there is the headroom. So, I think I will not say 70,
slightly below 70, very difficult to exactly put a number to it. Even in the aluminum side,
we are at operating at around 70%, 75%, but the order book is quite strong. So, we
need to increase CAPEX. We will quickly touch 80%, 85% by Q2 itself. So, by Q3, we
need CAPEX for the new orders which is already there in the pipeline.

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Yes, this is fine, sir. You can go ahead for the new business.

Management answered:
So, the new business now, our motive or whatever is the strategy behind the powertrain
business was, we were so heavily dependent on commercial vehicle earlier, very
reasonable extent on farm sector and also construction equipment and that was the
reason and little on the passenger vehicle segment. So, all that continues to be the
mainstay, but now our growth is going to come from not only the revival of these
segments, but also from the stationary engine side. We expect the first revenues to
come in, in FY27 not in '26. In '26, it will be there, the order book is filling up, but the
development cycle time is around 18 to 24 months. So, we will not see any three-digit
revenue, the three-digit revenue will only come in FY27 on the new powertrain business.
But as I mentioned, I think it will peak in 2029, 2030 for $100 million revenue for this
business. So, for that, the phase - I of Kothavadi plant is ready and it has become
operational in Q1. So, we will see some revenue generated from this plant. And the
machine shop for machining the large engine blocks are also ready, and already trials
are going on for various customers, orders are in place. So, you may appreciate these
are very few companies in the world are doing this as a Tier 1 supplier. The OEMs are
normally manufacturing them in-house and I would say less than 10 suppliers across
the globe, including China are there for this sort of activity and there was a limited
capacity. So, we have a global capability and global capacity now after the acquisition
of Fronberg Foundry. And in Europe also, on the foundry side also there are less than
half a dozen foundries who are operational for these large engine blocks and two or
three in the North American region and two or three in China. So, we are in exclusive
league in the foundry outside the OEMs themselves. As I mentioned, OEMs are also
running their own foundries, and the machining, and now because of the massive
increase in demand for the data centers, backup power and mainstream power also is
being used by these generators, the order books for these companies are quite full for
the next three, four years. So, we will see significant growth and that is also prompting
outsourcing, but whatever is said and done, the gestation period is very long. We started

Okay. And then secondly on the guidance, right, we have a very strong guidance on
powertrain, aluminum and even storage business. So, if I tie it up to the underlying be
it CV or PV industry, we may not see such high-volume growth. So, where is this
confidence coming for us to deliver 20%-plus kind of growth in an organic manner?

Management answered:
We have been diligently trying to diversify our customer portfolio. Earlier, we had six
customers giving 50% of our revenue and balance 50% of customers coming from equal
100 customers I would say. Now, the top 60% revenue is coming from 12 customers
and in diversified end customer segments. So, our product mix is like this and the
company side revenue, I mean consolidated if we look at it, the commercial vehicle
portion is coming to 17%, two wheeler is coming to 18%, passenger vehicle is 32%,
storage is 10%, off-highways is 5% and tractor is 4%. I think overall we have diversified
quite well and we have also invested for customers who are export-oriented. Many of
the OEMs from the eastern part of the world like Japan and Korea are also setting their
sights on African market for both passenger car, two-wheeler, pickup trucks, tractors
and all the products which are going to go into Africa in the future, which is the last
market to be developed, the products are similar to India, what India wanted in the last
two decades. But India is migrating to slightly higher products now. So, these products

results.question: What updates did management provide regarding new acquisitions, overseas offices, or capacity expansion?
results.expected_answer: Management discussed: (1) Fronberg Foundry (Germany) acquisition — completed and operational since 1st October 2024 as 'Craftsman Fronberg Guss GmbH'; investment stated in the Q3 call (30 Jan 2025) as INR 154 crores total (INR 94 crores for acquisition + INR 60 crores working capital), giving global foundry capability for the new stationary engine business, with first three-digit revenue expected in FY27 and business peaking at $100 million by 2029/2030. (2) DR Axion — acquisition of the balance 24% stake completed for INR 250 crores (Q3 call), making it a wholly-owned aluminium subsidiary. (3) Sunbeam — became a wholly-owned subsidiary from 9th October 2024, with investment of INR 606 crores as OCD (Q3 call); some of this to be recovered via the planned Gurgaon land sale (~Rs.300 crores). No new geography or product line beyond stationary engines/heavy engine castings and alloy wheels was discussed with a stated timeline/investment in the Q4 FY25 transcript.
results.answer:
## Details

- Regarding capacity expansion, management stated that the aluminum segment is currently operating at around 70-75% utilization, with plans to increase to 80-85% by Q2 FY26. To achieve this, they will need to increase CAPEX and by Q3 FY26, new CAPEX will be required for new orders already in the pipeline. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 6 · FY25)

- For the powertrain business, the phase-I of the Kothavadi plant has become operational in Q1 FY25. This plant, along with a machine shop for machining large engine blocks, is ready and undergoing trials with customers. This expansion supports the new powertrain business expected to generate significant revenue starting FY27 and peaking around FY29-FY30. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25)

- On acquisitions, management mentioned the acquisition of Fronberg Foundry, which has provided global capability and capacity in foundry operations for large engine blocks. This acquisition places the company in an exclusive league outside OEMs themselves. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 3 · FY25)

- Regarding overseas offices or export focus, management highlighted diversification of the customer portfolio, including export-oriented customers from Japan and Korea targeting the African market. However, no specific mention of new overseas offices was made. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Page 9 · FY25)

- On the Sunbeam acquisition, management noted that Sunbeam was taken over from an insolvency situation and is currently in a stabilization phase with no new order pipeline or revenue increase expected in the near term. (Source: crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 4-5 · FY25)

## Summary

Management provided updates on capacity expansion including ramping up aluminum segment utilization to 80-85% by Q2 FY26 with new CAPEX planned by Q3 FY26, and the operationalization of the Kothavadi plant and machine shop for large engine blocks in the powertrain segment. They also highlighted the acquisition of Fronberg Foundry, enhancing global foundry capabilities. While export-oriented customer diversification was noted, no new overseas offices were mentioned. The Sunbeam acquisition is in a stabilization phase with no immediate new orders or revenue growth.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 3, 4, 5, 6, 9 · FY25

results.question: What EBITDA margin contractions or fluctuations did management report, and what were the reasons?
results.expected_answer: Quantified margin impacts: In Q3 FY25, Sunbeam was EBITDA-positive but EBIT-negative at 'minus INR 9 crores'; the Bhiwadi plant had a negative EBIT of 'around INR 20 crores' due to start-up costs; combined, these two items impacted the Aluminium segment EBIT by 'around close to INR 30 crores' in Q3 FY25 alone (Jinesh Gandhi Q&A, Q3 call). Also in Q3 FY25, standalone 'Other expenses' (INR 148 crores) were flagged as one-time items from Sunbeam equipment transfer and aluminium alloy inventory rationalization to DR Axion. In Q4 FY25, alloy wheel was 'EBITDA-neutral and EBIT negative of around Rs.5 crores'; a one-off employee cost item in Germany (consolidated) was quantified at 'Rs.4.5 crores'.
results.answer:
## Details

From the earnings call transcript (crafts25Q4.pdf):

- Management mentioned a one-time impact leading to a reduction of Rs. 23 crores in EBITDA (Page 2).
- On the aluminum segment (Sunbeam), EBITDA margin for the quarter was around 6% to 7%. For FY26, management expects a blended EBITDA margin of 8% to 10%, with Q1 being weak, Q2 strong, Q3 weak, and Q4 the strongest due to plant shifting completion, consolidation, operating leverage, cost optimization, and manpower rationalization (Page 4).
- In the powertrain segment, margins improved in the quarter due to:
  - Completion of a modernization program and repair/maintenance expenses that had previously hit margins.
  - Improved operating leverage and capacity utilization.
  - Revival in farm and commercial vehicle sectors.
  - Stabilization of previous downside trends.
  - Margins expected to sustain or improve in FY26 compared to Q4 FY25 (Page 3).
- The industrial segment (storage business) saw a significant margin improvement in Q4 due to:
  - Low CAPEX and working capital requirements.
  - New orders and market traction.
  - Rationalization of product costing.
  - Good traction in static racking.
  - Management expects FY26 margins to be better than Q4 FY25, with some quarter-to-quarter variability (Page 9).
- The "Others" segment in standalone business showed a one-time impact due to:
  - CAPEX incurred by Sunbeam for equipment transfer (no profit or loss).
  - Rationalization of aluminum alloy inventory transferred at cost.
  - These are one-offs and not manufacturing income (Page 9).

## Summary

Management reported EBITDA margin fluctuations primarily due to one-time expenses totaling Rs. 23 crores impacting EBITDA. The aluminum segment experienced margin weakness in Q1 FY26 due to plant shifting but expects improvement to 8-10% EBITDA margin for the full year. Powertrain margins improved due to better operating leverage and sector revival, with expectations of sustained or improved margins in FY26. The industrial segment saw margin improvement from new orders and cost rationalization, with better margins expected in FY26. The "Others" segment had one-time impacts related to equipment transfers and inventory rationalization, causing margin fluctuations.

## Sources

crafts25Q4.pdf · Pages 2, 3, 4, 9 · FY25  
Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 19 · FY25

Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
And EBIT, EBITDA, sir?

Management answered:
No, that is the one-time some subjects are there, which is leading to Rs.23 crores
EBITDA.
Page 1 of 11

Analyst (Please go ahead.
Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Thanks for the opportunity and congrats for the strong set of numbers, sir. Sir, my first
question is on the aluminum side. So, on Sunbeam, how the quarterly run rate of
revenue and EBITDA margin would be in for 26? So, in this quarter we have done
around Rs.300 crores kind of the numbers and EBITDA is around 6% to 7%. So, how
this revenue and EBITDA would be in at the end of FY26?

Management answered:
So, Q1 will be weak, Q2 will be strong, Q3 will be weak, Q4 will be the most strong on
both on the revenue and EBITDA numbers. So, there are three, four factors. The plant
shifting from Gurgaon to Bhiwadi will be completed by Q2 latest I would say. So, there
will be consolidation happening in Q3. So, in Q4, the operating leverage at Bhiwadi will
set in and the cost optimization also will set in and also manpower rationalization will
be completed in Q4, also peak revenue will be generated in Q4. So, on a blended
average we can say between 8% to 10% EBITDA is what we expect for the full financial
year, but Q1 will be quite muted.

metadata.speaker:
Please go ahead.
Abhishek K Jain

chunks.content:
Analyst (Mukesh) asked:
Got it, sir. Good to see the improvement in the standalone margin. Sir, particularly in
the powertrain segment we have seen improvement this quarter. What led to the
improvement there? And also can you update how the Kothavadi plant order book is
shaping up for the heavy engines and for the FY26 and '27, how do you see the revenue
trends there?

Management answered:
Powertrain in the beginning, except the last quarter, I think for the last maybe almost
18 months, we went through quite a modernization program, repair and maintenance,
which was hitting the expenses I would say, that is one thing. The second thing is the
operating leverage was quite less, capacity utilization was quite less across the old
lines. New lines also were not ramping up. The customers were slow. So, now overall
slight improvement in the operating leverage has happened. That is helping us totally.
There has been from the multinational companies who set up plants in India they have
started to produce with the one- or two-years delay, I would say. And there is some
slight revival in the farm sector and a slight revival in the commercial vehicle sector and
whatever is the downside we had experiencing in the many quarters that has more or
less stabilized now, so that nothing more and whatever we are able to utilize the plants
slightly better and optimize our cost. These margins will be sustained or improved
depending on the quarter of the next financial year, but for the full financial year, we can
expect a better result on the powertrain, i.e., accumulative result even when compared
to the Q4 result. So, the margins will creep up just like the period before we did this
massive correction on the powertrain. So, any questions on the fundamental powertrain
business, then I will come to the new powertrain business.

Analyst (Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Got it, sir. Sir, this quarter, the storage, I mean the industrial segment margins have
seen a lot of improvement in Q4. Can you explain what led to the improvement there?
And going ahead for the next year how do you see the margin for this business, sir?

Management answered:
See, you may be aware of this, but I would like to reiterate one particular point on the
storage business. This is not at all CAPEX-intensive, there is CAPEX, but compared to
the automotive business on both aluminum and powertrain, this is a negligible CAPEX
I would say and working capital also the total capital employed is quite low. So, even
though margins may be low, once we see the traction what we got in Q4, which will
continue into the next financial year, of course, there can be some blips in quarter-to-
quarter but overall we can expect the results for FY26 will be better than the results of
Q4 of FY25. And the reason for that is the automated storage division is getting new
orders because we have made enough inroads into the market, we have enough
projects which have been implemented to showcase to our new incoming customers
and we are getting the right pricing for the product, #1. #2, I think we also have
rationalized the product costing and also on the static racking we are having good
traction there. So, as a blended Company, I think we have matured as a storage solution
Company. This is a very difficult business. Within five years we have come to a level.
This can be really a standalone also possible storage solution business. Not that I'm
implying anything, no, please don't take it that level, but we have grown this business
to be able to stand on its own feet now.

Analyst (Institutional Equities. Please go ahead.
Mumuksh Mandlesha) asked:
Thanks for taking the question again. Sir, in the segmental reporting, there is some line
called others in the standalone business which is around Rs.148 crores. Just can you
explain what is others in the standalone business, sir?

Management answered:
So, this is the one-time subject which has come on to two fronts. One is regarding some
CAPEX which has incurred by Sunbeam, we had to buy the equipment, keep everything
ready because Sunbeam was not ready at that level financially and we transferred the
brand-new machines to them totally. So, for Sunbeam no profit, no loss sale on the
equipment because equipment has to be paid for. Otherwise, we may not be able to do
the shifting of the plant. Second thing was, we rationalized the aluminum alloy inventory,
which was high in Craftsman by transferring at cost to DR Axion. These are the two
one-offs, not a transaction made for profit, it is not a manufacturing activity, so it cannot
be termed as any income.

metadata.speaker:
Institutional Equities. Please go ahead.
Mumuksh Mandlesha

chunks.content:
Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Okay, sir. And my last question is on the loan borrowing side. Now we have around
Rs.1,900 Crores kind of debt in books. So, what is your deleveraging plan for the next
two to three years?

Management answered:
So, if you look at it, the cash run rate generation will be quite high even in the next
financial year plus aided by possibly the land sale in Q3 or Q4 of Sunbeam, that will
add around Rs.300 crores plus depending on that market value at that particular point
of time. Our CAPEX guidance as a whole for a group will be only around Rs.750 crores
to Rs.800 crores totally in that region.

Analyst (Abhishek K Jain) asked:
Rs.750 crores in FY26?

Management answered:
Yes, on a group level and group level EBITDA of upward of Rs.1,100 crores and a land
sale of Rs.300 crores, depreciation is around Rs.450 crores, there will be some interest
outflow. And for Sunbeam there will be no tax, but for DR and Craftsman, there will be
taxes.

results.question: What is management's stance on debt reduction, cash utilization, and working capital normalization?
results.expected_answer: Debt: Consolidated net debt for FY25 projected at ~Rs.1,900 crores with debt-to-EBITDA of 2.24x (would have been 1.88x had the Gurgaon land sale of ~Rs.300 crores occurred in FY25). Management guided consolidated debt-to-EBITDA to come down to around 1.4x in FY26 (per the Q3 call), and in the Q4 call, debt was expected to reduce to 'INR 1,400 crores' at the consolidated level next year, aided by the land sale and lower capex. In the Q2 (Oct 2024) call, management stated a longer-term comfort zone of 1 to 1.5x debt-to-EBITDA, expecting to return to that range within 'one more year of operations.' No dividend payout policy or specific dividend figures were discussed anywhere in these transcripts — Not disclosed in the transcript.
results.answer:
## Summary  
Management is committed to reducing debt, utilizing cash primarily for acquisitions and debt repayment, and expects working capital to normalize with a significant reduction in the near term.

## Details  

### Debt Reduction  
- Management confirmed repayment of around ₹900 crores of debt in Q1 FY25, with ₹650 crores specifically allocated for debt reduction after accounting for acquisition and corporate use (crafts25Q1.pdf · Page 13 · FY25).  
- Despite capex and acquisition-related debt, management expects overall debt to reduce by 15-20% compared to the previous year (crafts23Q3.pdf · Page 13 · FY23).  
- Cash from fixed deposits is planned to be used either to close loans or pay for the DR Axion acquisition, indicating a clear focus on debt management (crafts23Q3.pdf · Page 7 · FY23).

### Cash Utilization  
- INR 250 crores of cash was used for the DR Axion acquisition, and about 25% (around INR 300 crores) was used for general corporate purposes, with the remainder directed towards debt repayment (crafts25Q1.pdf · Page 13 · FY25).  
- Management emphasized that the cash is real and earmarked for these specific uses, not just parked as fixed deposits (crafts23Q3.pdf · Page 7 · FY23).

### Working Capital Normalization  
- Working capital increased in FY22 due to strategic inventory build-up amid supply chain uncertainties and commodity price volatility (crafts22Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY22; crafts22Q3.pdf · Page 6 · FY22).  
- Management expects working capital to normalize with an anticipated reduction of around ₹80 crores in the current quarter and another ₹50 crores in Q1 FY23 (crafts22Q3.pdf · Page 6 · FY22).  
- Commodity price resets (20-30% on steel and aluminum) will still require factoring into working capital, but the overall trend is towards normalization (crafts22Q3.pdf · Page 6 · FY22).  
- Auto powertrain segment working capital is efficiently managed with minimal inventory and no warehouses, contributing to improved working capital cycle and ROCE (crafts22Q1.pdf · Page 20 · FY22).

## Sources  
crafts25Q1.pdf · Page 13 · FY25  
crafts23Q3.pdf · Pages 7, 13 · FY23  
crafts22Q4.pdf · Page 8 · FY22  
crafts22Q3.pdf · Page 6 · FY22  
crafts22Q1.pdf · Page 20 · FY22

chunks.content:
Management Commentary:
Yes, it will normalize.

Analyst (Dhaval Shah) asked:
So your gross will be how much, if you can give it?

Management answered:
See, the problem is, I don’t want to we have talked that acquisition cost of
something and we have taken some loans. So that FD is sitting there, that FD either
will be used to close loans or it will be used to pay for the DR Axion acquisition. So
this is real cash, not cash which is sitting as FD.

Management Commentary:
FY ’22 number?

Analyst (Joseph George) asked:
You mentioned that in FY22 there was a working capital hit because you
have to increase inventory. What I want to understand was next year do
you expect that to normalize and as a result from a cashflow perspective
do you expect working capital to release cash in FY23?

Management answered:
We are at least going to grow by 20% as I would say the guidance and
taking into consideration current market condition, the war, whatever
environment higher interest rates. So hopefully we'll do more than 20%.
Page 7 of 20

Analyst (Nishit Jalan) asked:
Sir, in your opening remarks you talked about working requirements being higher.
Even last quarter we saw our inventory levels are high because of strategic
purchase of certain raw material. So we are seeing a continuation of that even
now, and if you can share some numbers as to what is the scope of working
capital requirement going ahead and how it can help us leverage further our
balance sheet?

Management answered:
Wherever we are procuring material or maybe some time this is a straight pass-
through, sometimes it may be an indirect pass-through. We have seen not only
the basic commodity, but also the elements which is going into the basic
commodity like silicon and all going through a big troublesome phase of shortage
and it leads to quality issues, it leads to delivery issues. There are shipment
delays also which is causing lot of anxiety directly to us sometimes, directly to
our suppliers sometimes, sometimes a mismatch between the lead times which
was planned and which was there. And the uncertainty of customer volumes also
was hampering us. So, we took little strategic decisions on steel on the storage
business to increase inventory. Based on schedules of the other customers, we
had procured material, but that did not fructify into actual business. For certain
reason, the customers’ schedules drop. So, there is a temporary increase in
working capital requirement. We will see that in the next two quarters it will drop;
we expect around Rs.80 crores drop in the current quarter on the working capital
requirements and another Rs.50 crores in the Q1, this is what we expect. But
what we need to remember is the commodity price, there is a reset of around
20% to 30% both on steel as well as aluminum. That portion we have to factor in
our working capital requirements. Otherwise, the other correction will happen.
Page 5 of 20

Analyst (Senthilkumar) asked:
I have two questions. First one is, sir, this quarter we have repaid around of 900 crores
of debt, right? So, how much we can save in terms of interest costs in the forthcoming
quarter of FY '25?

Management answered:
Sir, the stated objective, whatever we have done is very clear. One is INR 250 Crore
was for the acquisition of DR Axion and it is very clear in the stated proceeds. And 25%
was for general corporate, which is around INR 300 Crore. So, only INR 650 Crore was
going to debt reduction.

Analyst (Disha Sheth) asked:
And sir, next year, we have a INR 350 crores, capex plan, plus the higher debt
which we took for acquisition. So sir, our cash flow would be affected, which would
again lead us to take more debt. So how are we managing that? Any views on
that?

Management answered:
I will wish to clarify that the EBITDA numbers for the nine-month figure is INR 499
crores totally. So you could annualize it or maybe it is slightly more than normal
because Q4 is always a better number, then we can apply some percentage which
we’ll do better than next year. So overall, I think the company will debt will reduce
next year.
Now when you look at the absolute debt, if you are minusing the DR Axion
acquisition payout, which will happen, then debt would have in real terms itself
would have reduced quite by 15%, 20% is what is my estimate compared to last
year in spite of the capex what we have done this year.
Page 12 of 21

Analyst (Amit Agrawal) asked:
So, would it be fair to say that these two businesses would account for like 75%-80% of the
working capital?

Management answered:
Yes correct. Auto powertrain will only constitute to 20% and here we also taken lot of efforts to
reduce the working capital requirement, we don't own any warehouses for the auto powertrain.
Wherever we are exporting directly also we export into the plant, so we don't have overseas
subsidies where we stock it on the auto powertrain. That is one thing we are efficiently able to
use the working capital cycle and this will also improve our ROCE. I think that is more
important. We don't see a huge requirement for working capital. Yes, for the commodity prices
putting some pressure on the working capital, otherwise we don't have a long supply chain.

results.question: What are the reportable business segments of the group, and what was their segment revenue for the current vs previous year?
results.expected_answer: The Company (standalone) has three reportable segments plus 'Others': Powertrain – revenue ₹1,68,272 lakhs (FY24-25) vs ₹1,55,837 lakhs (FY23-24); Aluminium Products – revenue ₹1,15,957 lakhs vs ₹91,746 lakhs; Industrial & Engineering – revenue ₹83,827 lakhs vs ₹73,196 lakhs; Others – revenue ₹16,739 lakhs vs Nil. Total revenue from operations: ₹3,84,795 lakhs (FY24-25) vs ₹3,20,779 lakhs (FY23-24). Segments are Powertrain, Aluminium Products, and Industrial & Engineering (Others is a residual category for insignificant segments).
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report 2024-25 (FY25), the reportable business segments of the group are identified based on end consumption of the products sold or services rendered. The segments are:

1. Powertrain  
2. Aluminium Products  
3. Industrial & Engineering  
4. Others (not significant segments)

Segment revenue data for the current year (FY25) and previous year (FY24) is provided in the notes to consolidated financial statements (Annual-Report-2025.pdf, pages 171, 172, 238):

- The exact segment revenue figures for FY25 and FY24 are disclosed in lakhs of rupees (₹ Lakhs).
- The total revenue as per the Statement of Profit and Loss matches the sum of segment revenues plus unallocable income/expenditure.

However, the exact numeric values for segment revenues for FY25 and FY24 are not explicitly stated in the retrieved context. The context mentions the segments and that segment revenue is disclosed, but the actual segment revenue numbers for each segment for FY25 and FY24 are not present in the retrieved data.

## Summary

The reportable business segments of the group are Powertrain, Aluminium Products, Industrial & Engineering, and Others. However, the exact segment revenue figures for the current year (FY25) and previous year (FY24) are not available in the retrieved context.

results.question: What key business risks does the company identify in its Risk Management framework, and what are their mitigation measures?
results.expected_answer: The Risk Management Committee maintains active oversight of risk and mitigation strategies. Identified key risks of the Company include: Strategic Risk, Operational Risk, Environment, Safety and Governance (ESG) Risk, and Information & Cyber Security Risk. The Company states it has a robust risk mitigation plan to minimize identified risks through continuous monitoring and mitigating actions.
results.answer:
## Details

The company identifies several key business risks in its Risk Management framework as outlined in the Annual Report FY25:

1. **Product Safety Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Product defects can lead to significant recalls, damage to brand equity, customer attrition, and regulatory penalties.  
   - **Mitigation:** Maintaining product safety and standards is crucial for customer trust and competitiveness. The company conducts regular safety audits, heavy incident investigations, trainings, risk assessments, provides PPE, safety training, emergency response plans, and obtains certifications like ISO 45001 for Occupational Health and Safety.

2. **Product Design & Lifecycle Management Opportunity**  
   - **Opportunity:** Integrating sustainability into product design to reduce environmental footprint, open new markets, and improve customer loyalty.  
   - **Mitigation:** Focus on products with better recyclability, lower emissions, and efficient end-of-life management to meet increasing regulations and customer expectations.

3. **Corporate Governance & Risk Management**  
   - **Risk:** Weak governance structures or inadequate risk management can expose the company to compliance violations, financial misstatements, and loss of investor confidence.  
   - **Mitigation:** The company emphasizes sound governance as crucial for long-term resilience.

4. **Occupational Health and Safety Risk**  
   - **Risk:** Health and safety issues in manufacturing such as accidents, exposure to hazardous materials, ergonomic issues, and regulatory sanctions.  
   - **Mitigation:** Conduct safety audits, provide PPE, training, and emergency response plans, and maintain certifications like ISO 45001.

5. **Climate Risk and Change**  
   - **Risk:** Physical risks from extreme weather events affecting manufacturing infrastructure, supply chains, and employee safety.  
   - **Opportunity:** Transition to a low-carbon economy presents opportunities to innovate with cleaner technologies, conserve energy and resources, lower operational costs, and access green and sustainability funds.  
   - **Mitigation:** Conduct energy audits, improve resource efficiency, and adopt cleaner technologies.

6. **Competition and Market Risks**  
   - **Risk:** Operating in competitive environments with customers maintaining multiple sources for products/services; cyclical nature of the automobile industry affected by interest rates and liquidity.  
   - **Mitigation:** Diversify products, services, markets, and customers; increase share of Industrial & Engineering segment to reduce cyclicality exposure; senior management monitors market and devises strategies to stay competitive.

7. **Financial Risks (Market Risk, Credit Risk, Liquidity Risk)**  
   - **Risk:** Exposure to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations, interest rate changes, credit risk, and liquidity risk.  
   - **Mitigation:** Use of derivative financial instruments to hedge foreign currency and interest rate risks; active management of currency exposures through treasury division; Board of Directors reviews and approves risk management policies and mitigation actions.

8. **Supply Chain and ESG Risks**  
   - **Risk:** Risks associated with supply chain disruptions and ESG compliance.  
   - **Mitigation:** Develop Supplier Code of Conduct emphasizing ESG criteria; conduct supplier ESG assessments and capacity-building initiatives; prioritize sourcing from local, certified, or low-carbon footprint vendors; track and monitor supplier ESG KPIs; enforce strong ethics, anti-corruption, and conflict of interest policies; conduct periodic ethics and compliance training; maintain transparent public disclosures aligned with legal and ESG reporting standards.

## Summary

The company identifies key business risks including product safety, occupational health and safety, corporate governance, climate change, competition, financial market risks, and supply chain ESG risks. Mitigation measures include rigorous safety audits and certifications, diversification of products and markets, use of derivatives for financial risk hedging, strong governance frameworks, supplier ESG assessments, and adoption of sustainable product design and cleaner technologies.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 6-7,  67-70,  156 · FY25

results.question: What transactions with related parties were disclosed in the financial statements, and what was the compensation of key management personnel?
results.expected_answer: Standalone related-party transactions for FY2024-25 (vs FY2023-24, ₹ in Lakhs) include: Purchase of Goods & Services – Subsidiaries ₹47 (₹70), Joint Ventures ₹501 (₹489); Sale of Goods & Services – Subsidiaries ₹19,304 (₹2,150), Joint Ventures ₹5,916 (₹5,597); Reimbursement of Expenditure to Subsidiaries ₹393 (Nil); Reimbursement of Expenditure from Subsidiaries ₹361 (Nil); Remuneration to Key Management Personnel ₹989 (₹692); Commission to KMP ₹149 (₹1,000); Sitting Fee ₹27 (₹22); Interest Income from Subsidiaries ₹346 (Nil); Rental income – Subsidiaries ₹67 (₹4), Joint Ventures ₹2 (₹2); Management Services Income – Subsidiaries ₹1,000 (Nil). Significant counterparties named include Carl Stahl Craftsman Enterprises Private Limited, Craftsman Europe BV-Netherlands, DR Axion India Private Limited, and Sunbeam Lightweighting Solutions Private Limited, with individual transaction values listed in the note (e.g., Sale of Goods & Services to DR Axion India Private Limited: ₹12,941 Lakhs; to Sunbeam Lightweighting Solutions Private Limited: ₹4,964 Lakhs). Balances outstanding at year-end (March 31, 2025) include Trade Receivables from Subsidiaries ₹888, Joint Ventures ₹983; Trade Payables to Subsidiaries ₹268; Loans Receivable from Subsidiaries ₹6,640 (marked with *); Remuneration payable to KMP ₹15.
results.answer:
## Details

- According to the Annual Report FY25 (pages 8, 16, 18, 21), the Company has stated that it **did not enter into any material related party transactions** during the financial year that would require shareholders’ approval under Listing Regulations or disclosure under Section 134(3)(h) of the Companies Act, 2013. All related party transactions were in the ordinary course of business and on an arm’s length basis.

- The related party transactions disclosed include:
  - Purchase of Goods & Services
  - Sale of Goods & Services
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure to and from related parties
  - Interest Income
  - Rental Income
  - Management Services Income
  - Commission and Sitting Fees paid to key management personnel

- Specific transaction values (in lakhs) during FY24-25 include (from page 164):
  - Purchase of Goods & Services: 4 lakhs
  - Sale of Goods & Services: 1930 lakhs
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure to related parties: 39 lakhs
  - Reimbursement of Expenditure from related parties: 36 lakhs
  - Interest Income: 34 lakhs
  - Rental Income: 6 lakhs
  - Management Services Income: 100 lakhs
  - Commission and Sitting Fees to KMP: Commission 989 lakhs, Sitting Fee 149 lakhs

- Key Management Personnel (KMP) identified (page 10) are:
  - Mr. Srinivasan Ravi, Chairman and Managing Director
  - Mr. Ravi Gauthamram, Whole Time Director
  - Mr. C.B. Chandrasekar, Chief Financial Officer
  - Mr. Thiyagaraj Damodharaswamy, Chief Operating Officer – Powertrain
  - Mr. Shainshad Aduvanni, Company Secretary

- The remuneration and other details of KMP for FY24-25 are provided in the Annual Return available on the Company’s website. However, the exact compensation figures for KMP are not explicitly disclosed in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The financial statements disclose related party transactions during FY24-25 including purchases and sales of goods and services, reimbursements, interest and rental income, management services income, and commission and sitting fees paid to key management personnel. All transactions were in the ordinary course of business and at arm’s length. The exact compensation amounts for key management personnel are not explicitly provided in the retrieved context.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 8, 10, 16, 18, 21, 164 · FY25

results.question: What contingent liabilities and commitments were reported by the company at the close of the financial year?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Contingent Liabilities as at March 31, 2025 (vs March 31, 2024, ₹ in Lakhs): a. Claims against the Company not acknowledged as debt – Excise Duty ₹7 (₹7), Service tax ₹104 (₹67), Goods and Service Tax ₹1,952 (₹16), Income tax ₹307 (₹823). b. Sales Bills discounted ₹8,646 (₹4,617). Additionally, Commitment on capital account not provided as on March 31, 2025 was ₹40,548 Lakhs (₹25,144 Lakhs as at March 31, 2024) — disclosed under Commitments in the same note.
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context from the provided sources (Excel financials, annual report images, PDF text, and earnings call transcripts) does not contain any explicit disclosure or mention of contingent liabilities or commitments reported by Craftsman Automation Ltd at the close of any financial year.

Specifically:

- The Excel sheets include detailed Profit & Loss, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow data but do not list contingent liabilities or commitments.
- The annual report image pages and PDF text sections provided do not include any narrative or tabular data on contingent liabilities or commitments.
- The earnings call transcripts do not mention contingent liabilities or commitments.

Therefore, no figures or descriptions of contingent liabilities or commitments are available in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The contingent liabilities and commitments reported by the company at the close of the financial year are not disclosed in the retrieved context.

results.question: What operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions did the company detail in the annual report?
results.expected_answer: The MD&A/Director's Report describes: (1) Development of a new off-highway components facility, described as 'a significant step towards scaling up its presence in this high-potential segment' (Powertrain segment); (2) Acquisition of assets of Fronberg Guss GmbH, bringing capacity, capabilities, a skilled workforce, and long-term customer relationships in Europe as part of the strategy to diversify geographically in iron castings and engineered systems; (3) Expansion of the Aluminium Products segment footprint through strategic inclusion of DR Axion and Sunbeam, enhancing machining and casting capabilities; (4) Commencement of cast iron manufacturing operations with green energy and machine tool parts. Outlook focus areas listed include optimizing capital structure through targeted debt reduction and diversifying revenue streams by growing the non-automotive portfolio. Separately, in the Notes to Standalone Financial Statements, Commitment on capital account not provided as on March 31, 2025 is stated as ₹40,548 Lakhs (₹25,144 Lakhs as at March 31, 2024).
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context from the Annual Report 2025 does not explicitly mention any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions undertaken by the company during the year. The available sections include:

- Financial performance highlights (Page 18) with turnover, PBT, PAT, and EBITDA figures.
- Notes on depreciation, amortization, and impairment (Pages 150, 217) which indicate capital asset usage but do not detail new capital expenditures or expansions.
- Key audit matters related to Property, Plant and Equipment (Page 108), which discuss accounting and valuation but do not describe new capacity or acquisitions.
- Segment reporting and joint venture accounting policies (Pages 191, 199) describe how segments and investments are accounted for but do not disclose new strategic acquisitions or expansions.
- Debt reduction and strategic focus points (Page 18) mention broad strategic goals such as maintaining EBITDA margins, improving profitability, diversifying revenue streams, and broadening the business base through strategic diversification initiatives, but no concrete details or figures on actual expansions or acquisitions are provided.

No explicit mention or detailed description of capital expenditure projects, capacity expansions, or completed/acquired strategic investments is found in the provided context.

## Summary

The annual report context provided does not disclose any specific operational capacity expansions, capital expenditures, or strategic acquisitions undertaken by the company during the year.

results.question: What Key Audit Matters (KAM) did the independent auditor identify, and what was the auditor's response to them?
results.expected_answer: In the Independent Auditor's Report on the Consolidated Financial Statements, two Key Audit Matters (KAMs) were raised: (1) 'Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment' — PPE (including CWIP) represents 48% of the Group's total assets; considered a KAM due to the material value and judgments/estimates involved (useful life, residual value, impairment/CGU assessment). Auditor's response: evaluated design and operating effectiveness of internal controls over initial recognition costs, valuation and useful lives, and physical verification; tested self-constructed assets on a sample basis; obtained management's technical confirmations for useful lives differing from Schedule II; reviewed residual values, CGU identification, discount factor computations, and impairment assumptions; physically inspected a sample of assets; reviewed physical verification reports. (2) 'Accounting for Business Combinations' — related to acquisition of 100% stake in Sunbeam Lightweighting Solutions Private Limited and Craftsman Germany GmbH (and its subsidiaries), involving complex judgments (acquirer/control determination, purchase price allocation, goodwill/bargain purchase recognition, and Ind AS 103 disclosures). Auditor's response: assessed acquisition agreements and control criteria under Ind AS 110/103; verified acquisition dates; reviewed purchase consideration calculations including contingent consideration; assessed fair valuation methodologies for purchase price allocation; verified goodwill/bargain purchase computation; reviewed disclosure compliance with Ind AS 103. Note: the Standalone Financial Statements' Auditor's Report lists only the PPE matter as a Key Audit Matter (not Business Combinations).
results.answer:
## Details

The independent auditor identified the following Key Audit Matters (KAM) for the current period audit of Craftsman Automation Limited:

1. **Accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE)**  
   - The capitalisation of PPE includes the fair value of the assets acquired (including rebates) and any directly attributable costs of bringing the asset to its working condition, excluding non-refundable purchase taxes and duties.  
   - Depreciation is charged as per Schedule II to the Companies Act, 2013.  
   - The audit procedures included:  
     - Testing controls and operating effectiveness of internal controls over PPE accounting.  
     - Verifying the capitalisation of self-constructed PPE.  
     - Assessing depreciation rates applied.  
     - Reviewing management’s impairment assessment and valuation judgments.  
     - Examining underlying documentation for compliance with accounting standards.

2. **Accounting for Business Combinations**  
   - The audit focused on:  
     - Assessing acquisition agreements and verifying acquisition dates.  
     - Reviewing the calculation of total purchase consideration and evaluating fair value measurements of identifiable assets and liabilities acquired.  
     - Assessing methodologies and assumptions used in valuation.  
     - Verifying computation of goodwill or gain on bargain purchase and reasonableness of assumptions.  
     - Reviewing disclosures in consolidated financial statements for compliance with Ind AS 103 (Business Combinations).

The auditor’s response to these KAMs involved detailed substantive procedures and controls testing to ensure the accuracy and compliance of these complex accounting areas with applicable Indian Accounting Standards (Ind AS) and the Companies Act, 2013.

Additionally, the auditor reported that:

- Proper books of accounts have been maintained.  
- The standalone financial statements give a true and fair view in conformity with Ind AS and other accounting principles generally accepted in India.  
- The company has disclosed the impact of pending litigations, made provisions for material foreseeable losses on long-term contracts including derivatives, and there were no amounts due to be transferred to the Investor Education and Protection Fund.  
- The auditor communicated significant audit findings and any deficiencies in internal control to those charged with governance.

## Summary

The independent auditor identified two Key Audit Matters: (1) accounting for Property, Plant and Equipment, focusing on capitalisation, depreciation, and impairment judgments; and (2) accounting for Business Combinations, focusing on acquisition accounting and fair value measurements. The auditor responded with thorough audit procedures including controls testing, substantive verification, and compliance review, concluding that the financial statements present a true and fair view in accordance with applicable standards.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Pages 108-111, 175-182 · FY25

results.question: What segment profit, segment assets, and segment liabilities were reported for the company's core segments?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Segment Profit or Loss (₹ in Lakhs), FY2024-25 vs FY2023-24: Powertrain ₹24,857 (₹29,163); Aluminium Products ₹11,600 (₹12,902); Industrial & Engineering ₹1,717 (₹4,673); Others ₹248 (Nil); Total Segments ₹38,422 (₹46,738). Segment Assets as at March 31, 2025 vs March 31, 2024: Powertrain ₹1,76,711 (₹1,54,572); Aluminium Products ₹1,66,955 (₹1,09,219); Industrial & Engineering ₹1,09,880 (₹86,324); Total Segments ₹4,53,546 (₹3,50,115). Segment Liabilities: Powertrain ₹1,01,851 (₹94,667); Aluminium Products ₹1,24,967 (₹59,650); Industrial & Engineering ₹72,432 (₹59,106); Total Segments ₹2,99,250 (₹2,13,423). Note: The document discloses Segment Profit or Loss, Segment Assets, and Segment Liabilities, but does not separately disclose 'segment-wise capital employed' as a distinct line item — Not disclosed in this annual report under that specific term.
results.answer:
## Details

The core segments of the company as per the annual report are:

- Powertrain
- Aluminium Products
- Industrial & Engineering

From the Annual Report 2024-25 (FY25), the reported figures for these segments are:

## Summary

For FY25, the company reported segment profits of ₹24,857 lakhs (Powertrain), ₹11,600 lakhs (Aluminium Products), and ₹1,717 lakhs (Industrial & Engineering). Segment assets were ₹1,76,711 lakhs, ₹1,66,955 lakhs, and ₹1,09,880 lakhs respectively, while segment liabilities stood at ₹1,01,851 lakhs, ₹1,24,967 lakhs, and ₹72,432 lakhs for these core segments.

results.question: What are the top three largest line items under 'Other Expenses' in the standalone financial statements, and how did they change YoY?
results.expected_answer: Standalone 'Other Expenses' (Note 2.7) for FY2024-25 vs FY2023-24 (₹ in Lakhs) — the three largest individual line items in FY2024-25 were: (1) Stores, Spares & tool consumed: ₹24,796 vs ₹20,696, an increase of ₹4,100; (2) Power & Fuel: ₹21,548 vs ₹17,710, an increase of ₹3,838; (3) Payment to contractors: ₹8,441 vs ₹5,849, an increase of ₹2,592. Total Other Expenses were ₹88,833 Lakhs (FY24-25) vs ₹72,243 Lakhs (FY23-24).
results.answer:
## Details

The retrieved context provides detailed line items for the profit and loss statement of Craftsman Automation Ltd for multiple years, including a line item called "Other Expenses." However, the context only shows a single aggregated figure for "Other Expenses" each year and does not break down "Other Expenses" into sub-line items or components.

For example, the "Other Expenses" figures from the profit and loss data are:

- FY2017: 7.79 Cr
- FY2018: 6.23 Cr
- FY2019: 6.37 Cr
- FY2020: 3.74 Cr
- FY2021: 3.14 Cr
- FY2022: 2.09 Cr
- FY2023: 4.11 Cr
- FY2024: 5.64 Cr
- FY2025: 18.98 Cr
- FY2026: 1842.44 Cr (Note: This is a very large jump in FY2026)

No further breakdown or sub-classification of "Other Expenses" into specific line items is provided in the retrieved context.

## Summary

The retrieved context does not disclose the top three largest line items under "Other Expenses" in the standalone financial statements, nor does it provide their year-on-year changes. Only the aggregate "Other Expenses" figure is available for each year.

## Sources

Craftsman Auto.xlsx · profit_loss · FY2017 to FY2026

results.question: What new or amended accounting standards were disclosed, and what was their impact?
results.expected_answer: The document states: 'During the year ended March 31, 2025, MCA has not notified any new standards or amendments to the existing standards applicable to the company.' No accounting policy changes or new accounting standards were adopted during the financial year, and no impact amount is quantified since none were adopted.
results.answer:
## Details

From the Annual Report FY25, page 123, under the section "C. RECENT ACCOUNTING DEVELOPMENTS," it is explicitly stated:

- During the year ended March 31, 2025, the Ministry of Corporate Affairs (MCA) has **not notified any new accounting standards or amendments to the existing standards applicable to the company**.

No impact or changes due to new or amended accounting standards were disclosed.

## Summary

No new or amended accounting standards were notified or applicable to the company during FY25, and therefore, no impact from such standards was disclosed.

## Sources

Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 123 · FY25

results.question: What provisions (current and non-current) for employee benefits or other liabilities were disclosed, and what schedules were provided?
results.expected_answer: Standalone Short Term Provisions (Note 1.32) as at March 31, 2025 vs March 31, 2024 (₹ in Lakhs): Provision for employee benefits — Gratuity ₹630 (₹505); Other Provisions — Provision for Warranty Cost ₹280 (₹230), Provision for Rejection ₹160 (₹127); Total ₹1,070 (₹862). Movement in Provision for product warranties (FY2024-25): Opening ₹230, Expired during the year ₹230, Warranty provided for current year ₹280, Closing ₹280. Movement in Provision for Rejection (FY2024-25): Opening ₹127, Expired during the year ₹127, Rejection provided for current year ₹160, Closing ₹160. Additionally, Note 2.7 'Other Expenses' discloses Provisions charged during the year: Warranty ₹45 (₹41 in FY23-24), Rejections ₹34 (₹(3) in FY23-24), Doubtful debts ₹933 (₹(94) in FY23-24).
results.answer:
## Details

From the retrieved context, the provisions for employee benefits and other liabilities disclosed are as follows:

### Non-current Provisions (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 145 · FY25)
- Other Provisions (including Warranty Cost)
- Provision for compensated absences

### Current Provisions (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147 · FY25)
- Provision for employee benefits - Gratuity: ₹630 Lakhs (FY25), ₹505 Lakhs (FY24)
- Other Provisions:
  - Provision for Warranty Cost: ₹280 Lakhs (FY25), ₹230 Lakhs (FY24)
  - Provision for Rejection: ₹160 Lakhs (FY25), ₹127 Lakhs (FY24)
- Total Current Provisions: ₹1,070 Lakhs (FY25), ₹862 Lakhs (FY24)

### Movement in Provisions (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147 · FY25)
- Warranty Provision:
  - Opening: ₹230 Lakhs
  - Expired during the year: ₹230 Lakhs
  - Provided during the year: ₹280 Lakhs
  - Closing: ₹280 Lakhs
- Rejection Provision:
  - Opening: ₹127 Lakhs
  - Expired during the year: ₹127 Lakhs
  - Provided during the year: ₹160 Lakhs
  - Closing: ₹160 Lakhs

### Provisions Charged During the Year (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 147 · FY25)
- Warranty: ₹45 Lakhs (FY25), ₹41 Lakhs (FY24)
- Rejections: ₹34 Lakhs (FY25), -₹3 Lakhs (FY24)
- Doubtful debts: ₹933 Lakhs (FY25), -₹94 Lakhs (FY24)

### Notes on Provisions (Annual-Report-2025.pdf · Page 129 · FY25)
- Provisions for warranty obligations are recognized based on management’s best estimate of expenditure required to settle the obligation.

### Schedules Provided
- Note 1.24 Long Term Provisions (Non-current provisions)
- Note 1.32 Short Term Provisions (Current provisions)
- Movement schedules for warranty and rejection provisions are detailed.
- Provisions charged during the year are disclosed in Note 2.7 Other Expenses.

## Summary

The company disclosed both current and non-current provisions related to employee benefits and other liabilities. Current provisions include gratuity (₹630 Lakhs), warranty cost (₹280 Lakhs), and rejection (₹160 Lakhs) as of March 31, 2025. Non-current provisions include compensated absences and warranty provisions. Detailed schedules for movements in warranty and rejection provisions, as well as provisions charged during the year, are provided in Notes 1.24, 1.32, and 2.7 of the annual report.

results.question: Compute the Debt-to-Equity ratio for FY25 and FY26 and assess the change in financial leverage.
results.expected_answer: FY25 D/E = 0.83x. FY26 D/E = 1.11x. Borrowings rose 54% in one year while equity grew only 14%, indicating accelerating debt-funded capex.
results.answer:
## Summary
The Debt-to-Equity ratio increased from 0.83 in FY25 to 1.11 in FY26, indicating a rise in financial leverage.

## Details
- FY25 Borrowings = ₹2358.18 million  
- FY25 Networth = ₹2856.74 million  
- FY26 Borrowings = ₹3623.48 million  
- FY26 Networth = ₹3264.06 million  

## Calculation
Debt-to-Equity ratio = Borrowings / Networth

- FY25: 2358.18 / 2856.74 = 0.83  
- FY26: 3623.48 / 3264.06 = 1.11  

This shows an increase in the Debt-to-Equity ratio by 0.28 (1.11 - 0.83), reflecting higher financial leverage in FY26 compared to FY25.

## Sources

results.question: Calculate the Operating Profit Margin for FY24 and FY25 and comment on the trend. OPM = Operating Profit / Sales × 100
results.expected_answer: FY24 OPM: 19.74%. FY25 OPM: 14.64%. OPM contracted sharply by ~510 bps despite 27.8% revenue growth — a significant margin deterioration.
results.answer:
## Summary
The Operating Profit Margin (OPM) decreased from 10.13% in FY24 to 5.12% in FY25, indicating a decline in operating profitability relative to sales.

## Details
- FY24:
  - EBITDA = ₹728.61
  - Depreciation = ₹277.69
  - Sales = ₹4451.73
- FY25:
  - EBITDA = ₹638.35
  - Depreciation = ₹347.02
  - Sales = ₹5690.48

## Calculation
Operating Profit = EBITDA - Depreciation

For FY24:  
Operating Profit = 728.61 - 277.69 = ₹450.92  
OPM = (Operating Profit / Sales) × 100 = (450.92 / 4451.73) × 100 = 10.13%

For FY25:  
Operating Profit = 638.35 - 347.02 = ₹291.33  
OPM = (291.33 / 5690.48) × 100 = 5.12%

## Sources
Craftsman Auto.xlsx · calculation · 2025
